docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option A — One-line installer (easiest)
docs.body: Option A — One-line installer (easiest) Requires a public repo and curl : curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh Pin a specific tag: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh -s -- --version 1.0.0 Reload shell: source ~/.zshrc # or ~/.bashrc nyra --version

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option B — Manual install from a release tag
docs.body: Option B — Manual install from a release tag Download nyra-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz or nyra-x86_64-darwin.tar.gz from Releases . Extract: mkdir -p ~/.nyra tar -xzf ~/Downloads/nyra-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz -C ~/.nyra Add to ~/.zshrc (or ~/.bash_profile ): export NYRA_HOME="$HOME/.nyra" export PATH="$NYRA_HOME/bin:$PATH" If macOS blocks the binary ( killed on first run): xattr -cr ~/.nyra/bin/nyra Verify: source ~/.zshrc nyra --version

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 5. Linux
docs.body: 5. Linux

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option A — One-line installer
docs.body: Option A — One-line installer curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh # or pin version: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh -s -- --version 1.0.0 source ~/.bashrc # or ~/.zshrc nyra --version

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 6. Windows
docs.body: 6. Windows Prebuilt Windows binaries ship on every release tag as nyra-x86_64-windows.zip .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option A — PowerShell installer (recommended)
docs.body: Option A — PowerShell installer (recommended) irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.ps1 | iex # or pin version: .\scripts\install.ps1 -Version 1.1.0 nyra --version Requires clang (LLVM for Windows) on PATH to link .ny programs.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option B — Manual zip from Releases
docs.body: Option B — Manual zip from Releases Download nyra-x86_64-windows.zip from Releases . Extract to e.g. %USERPROFILE%\.nyra . Add %USERPROFILE%\.nyra\bin to PATH and set NYRA_HOME . Dot-source env.ps1 in the zip or open a new terminal after the installer updated user PATH.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Option C — WSL2 (Ubuntu)
docs.body: Option C — WSL2 (Ubuntu) Install WSL2 and Ubuntu. Inside WSL, install clang: sudo apt install clang curl tar . Follow Linux (§5) — installer or manual tarball nyra-x86_64-linux.tar.gz . Create projects under your Linux home (e.g. ~/projects/myapp ).

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Alternative — Build from source (native Windows)
docs.body: Alternative — Build from source (native Windows) Install Rust and clang (LLVM) on Windows. Clone the repo (same tag you want, e.g. v1.0.0 ): git clone https://github.com/nyra-lang/nyra.git cd nyra git checkout v1.0.0 cargo build --release -p nyra-cli Use the built CLI and point runtime at the repo stdlib : $env:PATH = "$PWD\target\release;$env:PATH" $env:NYRA_HOME = "$PWD" .\target\release\nyra.exe --version Run examples from the repo root: .\target\release\nyra.exe run examples\syntax\hello.ny Native Windows support is evolving; WSL2 matches the Linux release workflow today.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Install nyrapkg (package manager)
docs.body: Install nyrapkg (package manager) Dependency management uses the standalone nyrapkg tool ( guide · github.com/nyra-lang/pkg ), installed alongside nyra under ~/.nyra/bin/ : curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/pkg/main/scripts/install.sh | sh nyrapkg --version From source: nyra build --release -o nyrapkg . in the pkg repo, then nyrapkg bootstrap .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 7. First project (all platforms)
docs.body: 7. First project (all platforms) Once nyra --version works (e.g. nyra 1.0.0 ): mkdir myapp cd myapp nyrapkg init This creates: nyra.mod , nyra.lock , nyra.sum main.ny with a Hello, world program Run it: nyra run main.ny Expected output: Hello, world! Multi-file projects For apps with import , run from the project root: nyra run . — see Modules and Getting started . Other useful commands: nyra check nyra build nyra build --release nyra fmt --write main.ny

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 8. Upgrade to a newer tag
docs.body: 8. Upgrade to a newer tag Open Releases and pick a newer tag (e.g. v0.1.2 ). macOS/Linux: re-run the install script with --version , or download the new .tar.gz and extract over ~/.nyra . Confirm: nyra --version .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 9. Build from source (contributors)
docs.body: 9. Build from source (contributors) If you work on the compiler itself: git clone https://github.com/nyra-lang/nyra.git cd nyra cargo build --release -p nyra-cli export PATH="$PWD/target/release:$PATH" nyra run examples/syntax/hello.ny Or: cargo install --path cli Local iteration: ./scripts/dev-install.sh from the repo root.

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 10. Uninstall
docs.body: 10. Uninstall rm -rf ~/.nyra Remove the NYRA_HOME / PATH lines from ~/.zshrc , ~/.bashrc , or ~/.bash_profile .

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: 11. Troubleshooting
docs.body: 11. Troubleshooting Problem Fix clang not found Install clang (see §1 ) release asset not found Tag not published yet, wrong arch asset, or private repo without login Runtime not found Set export NYRA_HOME=~/.nyra and ensure share/stdlib/nyra_rt.c exists zsh: killed (macOS) xattr -cr ~/.nyra/bin/nyra Failed to read main.ny Run nyrapkg init first, or create main.ny 404 on raw.githubusercontent.com Repo is private — use Releases download or make repo public

docs.title: Install Nyra
docs.heading: Quick reference
docs.body: Quick reference Step Command Latest install (mac/Linux, public repo) curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/nyra/main/scripts/install.sh | sh Pin version ... | sh -s -- --version 1.0.0 Package manager curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nyra-lang/pkg/main/scripts/install.sh | sh New project nyrapkg init && nyra run . Releases page github.com/nyra-lang/nyra/releases Canonical source: install.md in the repository. Getting started → · Overview

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Nyra Keywords
docs.body: Nyra Keywords Every reserved word and type name the lexer treats as a keyword — plus how to reuse those names as variables with a leading @ , and how compiler attributes differ ( #[…] , not @ ). On this page @ vs #[ ] Raw identifiers ( @name ) Reserved words list Bindings & constants Control flow Types, modules & functions Memory & ownership Concurrency & async Type names Compiler attributes ( #[…] ) Quick reference

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: @ vs #[ ]
docs.body: @ vs #[ ] Nyra uses two different prefix forms. They are not interchangeable. Syntax Role Example @name Raw identifier — use a reserved word or type name as a variable / field name let @module = 10 #[attr] Compiler attribute — metadata on functions, structs, or parameters #[derive(Copy)] module … Keyword — declare a module path module my.app clone x Keyword — explicit heap duplicate at a call site save(clone user) @module and @clone in source are not special builtins — they are ordinary identifiers whose names happen to match keywords. See Raw identifiers below.

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Raw identifiers ( @name )
docs.body: Raw identifiers ( @name ) When a name is reserved (keyword or built-in type), you cannot use it bare as an identifier. Prefix it with @ — the same idea as Rust r#name . The lexer strips the @ and treats the token as a normal Identifier . The compiler stores the name without the prefix. Without types With types fn main() { let @module = 10 let @clone = @module print(@clone) // prints 10 } fn main() -> void { let @module = 10 let @clone = @module print(@clone) // prints 10 } Here @module is a variable named module ; @clone is a variable named clone . This is unrelated to the module declaration keyword or the clone ownership prefix.

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: When @ is required
docs.body: When @ is required Any name that the lexer would otherwise parse as a keyword or type token needs @ to be used as an identifier: Without @ With @ module → module keyword @module → variable named module clone → clone prefix @clone → variable named clone fn → function keyword @fn → variable named fn i32 → type i32 @i32 → variable named i32

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Rules
docs.body: Rules @ must be followed by a letter or _ (not a digit). let x = @ alone is a lexer error ( Expected identifier after '@' ). let x = @1 is invalid for the same reason. C bindings from nyra pkg c add may append _ to reserved C names instead of using @ (see C Bindgen ).

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Reserved words list
docs.body: Reserved words list The table below matches the compiler’s reserved-name list used by C bindgen ( c-bindgen/src/names.rs ). These names cannot appear as bare identifiers in Nyra source. let mut fn if else while return true false print import module struct impl self for const extern export inst enum match spawn in test async await trait macro defer unsafe asm as move clone void bool string ptr char type out i8 i16 i32 i64 i128 u8 u16 u32 u64 u128 isize usize f64 The lexer also reserves additional tokens (e.g. break , continue , pub , priv , dyn , f32 , benchmark , parallel , progress ). Treat any keyword from the language reference as requiring @ when used as an identifier.

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Bindings & constants
docs.body: Bindings & constants Keyword Purpose let Immutable binding — value cannot be reassigned mut Used with let mut for a reassignable binding const Compile-time constant name true / false Boolean literals

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Control flow
docs.body: Control flow Keyword Purpose if / else Conditional branches while Loop while condition is true for / in Iteration — for i in 0..10 or for x in arr match Pattern match on enums and values return Return from the current function

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Types, modules & functions
docs.body: Types, modules & functions Keyword Purpose fn Function definition struct Product type with named fields enum Sum type with variants impl Methods on a type; impl Trait for Type trait Trait definition (Extended) self Method receiver inside impl import Load another .ny file module Module path declaration — see Modules extern Declare an external (C) function export Export a symbol for FFI inst Generic instance / monomorph hook (tooling) type Type alias or associated type surface macro Macro definition (Extended) test Test function — run with nyra test print Built-in stdout; optional color: for ANSI output as Type cast — expr as Type unsafe Block for raw pointers and low-level operations asm Inline assembly (inside unsafe ) defer Scope-exit call (LIFO) — Extended; prefer impl Drop out Output / FFI direction marker in bindings

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Memory & ownership
docs.body: Memory & ownership Keyword Purpose move Unary prefix at a call site — transfer ownership explicitly ( guide ) clone Unary prefix or .clone() — heap duplicate; original stays valid clone user and let @clone = user mean completely different things: the first duplicates a value; the second binds a variable whose name is the word clone .

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Concurrency & async
docs.body: Concurrency & async Keyword Purpose spawn Start a concurrent block async / await Async functions and suspension (Extended)

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Type names
docs.body: Type names These tokens are parsed as type names, not ordinary identifiers. Use @ if you need a variable with the same spelling. Type Notes i8 … i128 Signed integers; i32 is the default for int literals u8 … u128 Unsigned integers isize , usize Pointer-sized integers f64 IEEE-754 double; default float literal type char Unicode scalar — 'a' , '\n' bool true / false string UTF-8 heap string (Move) void Functions with no return value ptr Opaque FFI pointer handle Full type reference: Types & data · Language reference → Type names

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Compiler attributes ( #[…] )
docs.body: Compiler attributes ( #[…] ) Attributes attach to items in source. They start with # , not @ . Attribute Purpose #[derive(Copy)] Document / validate that a struct is Copy-safe #[derive(Clone)] Synthesize .clone() when fields allow it #[comptime] Evaluate this function at compile time; strip from binary — Comptime guide #[inline] Hint inlining at call sites #[hot] / #[cold] LLVM branch-hint attributes for hot / cold paths #[no_escape] On &T parameters — value must not escape the function — Escape analysis Without types With types #[derive(Copy)] struct Point { x: i32 y: i32 } #[comptime] fn table_size() { return 256 } fn process(#[no_escape] data) { ... } #[derive(Copy)] struct Point { x: i32 y: i32 } #[comptime] fn table_size() -> i32 { return 256 } fn process(#[no_escape] data: i32) { ... }

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: Quick reference
docs.body: Quick reference You write Meaning @module Variable (or field) named module module my.app Declare module path my.app @clone Variable named clone clone user Duplicate user for a call; keep user valid #[derive(Copy)] Compiler attribute on a struct @i32 Variable named i32 (not type annotation)

docs.title: Nyra Keywords
docs.heading: LLVM @ (not Nyra source)
docs.body: LLVM @ (not Nyra source) Generated LLVM IR uses @symbol for global functions (e.g. @i32_to_string ). That is LLVM notation, not something you write in .ny files. Language reference →

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Language basics
docs.body: Language basics Learn Nyra from zero — no prior programming experience required. Every keyword is explained in plain language with runnable examples. New to programming? Start the Beginner track — eight guided lessons with line-by-line explanations, try-it exercises, and common mistakes. Use this page as a reference once you finish the track. Who is this for? If you have never written code before, start here. We explain what each piece of syntax means and why you would use it — not just how to type it. On this page Program structure Variables overview let — bind a name mut — mutable (changeable) const — constants let vs mut vs const Types Functions Loops Conditionals Structs Enums & match Imports Methods Built-in I/O Next steps

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Program structure
docs.body: Program structure A program is a list of instructions the computer runs from top to bottom. In Nyra, the entry point is always a function called main . Without types With types fn main() { print("Hello, Nyra!") } fn main() -> void { print("Hello, Nyra!") } fn main() — defines the function named main . When you run the program, execution starts here. { … } — a block . Code inside the block runs together. Indentation helps you read it; Nyra does not require specific spacing. print(...) — sends text or numbers to the screen (standard output). // comment — notes for humans; the compiler ignores everything after // on that line. Try it: save as hello.ny , then run nyra run hello.ny . For a folder with main.ny , run nyra run . from that folder.

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Variables overview
docs.body: Variables overview A variable is a name you give to a value so you can use it later — like labeling a box with what is inside. Without types With types let age = 25 print(age) // prints 25 let age: i32 = 25 print(age) // prints 25 Here age is the name, and 25 is the value stored under that name. When the program runs, Nyra remembers that age means 25 . Nyra gives you three main ways to introduce names: let — a normal binding; the value should not be replaced later. let mut — a binding you can change later (counters, accumulators, game state). const — a fixed name for a value known when the program is compiled.

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: let — bind a name (immutable)
docs.body: let — bind a name (immutable) let creates a variable and sets its initial value. By default the binding is immutable : after the first assignment, you must not assign a new value to that name. Without types With types let player_name = "Nyra" let score = 10 let pi_approx = 3 print(score) // OK — reading is always fine let player_name: string = "Nyra" let score: i32 = 10 let pi_approx: i32 = 3 print(score) // OK — reading is always fine Why immutable by default? When a value never changes, the compiler and other readers of your code can reason about it more easily. Most variables in a program never need to change. Type inference: Nyra figures out the type from the value. 10 is an i32 (integer). "Nyra" is a string . You can write the type explicitly if you want: let score: i32 = 10 . What if you try to change it? Without types With types let score = 10 score = 20 // compile error — score is not mut let score: i32 = 10 score = 20 // compile error — score is not mut Nyra stops at compile time with an error telling you to use let mut if the value must change.

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: mut — short for mutable
docs.body: mut — short for mutable mut is an abbreviation of mutable — meaning the variable is allowed to change after it is created. You use it when the value will be updated later (loops, counters, running totals). Without types With types let mut lives = 3 lives = lives - 1 // OK — lives is mutable print(lives) // 2 let mut lives: i32 = 3 lives = lives - 1 // OK — lives is mutable print(lives) // 2 Think of let mut as: “create a named box, and I will put different values in it over time.”

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Shorthand: mut without let
docs.body: Shorthand: mut without let In some examples you will see mut sum = 0 instead of let mut sum = 0 . Both declare a mutable variable; the shorter form appears in loops and benchmarks. Without types With types fn main() { mut counter = 0 counter = counter + 1 print(counter) // 1 } fn main() -> void { mut counter = 0 counter = counter + 1 print(counter) // 1 }

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: When you need mut
docs.body: When you need mut Without types With types let mut i = 0 while i < 3 { print(i) i = i + 1 // i must be mut — we change it every loop } let mut i: i32 = 0 while i < 3 { print(i) i = i + 1 // i must be mut — we change it every loop } Without mut , the line i = i + 1 would be rejected — you cannot reassign an immutable let .

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: const — compile-time constants
docs.body: const — compile-time constants const defines a constant : a name for a value that is fixed for the entire program and known when you compile (not computed at runtime). Without types With types const MAX_PLAYERS = 4 const GRID_SIZE = 8 fn main() { print(MAX_PLAYERS) } const MAX_PLAYERS = 4 const GRID_SIZE = 8 fn main() -> void { print(MAX_PLAYERS) } let let mut const Can reassign? No Yes No Value set when? At that line in main (or any function) At that line; can change later At compile time (fixed) Typical use Most values that do not change Counters, loops, game state Limits, config names, magic numbers Example let name = "Ali" let mut hp = 100 const MAX_HP = 100

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: let vs mut vs const — side by side
docs.body: let vs mut vs const — side by side Without types With types const MAX = 100 // never changes; known at compile time let title = "Dungeon" // set once at runtime; do not reassign // title = "Other" // ERROR let mut gold = 0 // starts at 0; we will add to it gold = gold + 15 // OK print(gold) // 15 const MAX = 100 // never changes; known at compile time let title = "Dungeon" // set once at runtime; do not reassign // title = "Other" // ERROR let mut gold: i32 = 0 gold = gold + 15 // OK print(gold) // 15 Rule of thumb: use let unless you need to reassign — then use let mut . Use const for shared fixed numbers used in many places. Heap string follows Move rules; structs with only number fields are auto-Copy . Calls often auto-borrow — see Ownership UX and Memory & ownership . Repo example: examples/comparison/arithmetic/sum.ny

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Types — what kind of value is it?
docs.body: Types — what kind of value is it? A type tells Nyra what operations are allowed. You cannot add a string to an integer; the type checker catches mistakes before the program runs. Without types With types let n = 0 // integer (32-bit) let ok = true // true or false let msg = "hi" let n: i32 = 0 let ok = true // true or false let msg: string = "hi" Usually Nyra infers the type so you can omit : i32 . Float literals like 3.14 infer f64 . Add annotations when it helps readability or the compiler needs a hint. Type Example value Used for i32 42 , -1 Whole numbers (scores, counts) f64 3.14 , 19.99 Decimals (price, rating, lat/lng, progress) bool true , false Yes/no decisions in if and loops string "hello" Text void — “This function returns nothing” struct Point { x: 1, y: 2 } Grouping related fields (like a small object) enum Color.Red A fixed set of named variants Full reference: Types & data .

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Functions — reusable pieces of code
docs.body: Functions — reusable pieces of code A function is a named block of code you can call whenever you need it. Functions avoid repeating the same logic and make programs easier to read. Without types With types fn add(a, b) { return a + b } fn greet(name) { print(name) } fn main() { let total = add(10, 32) print(total) // 42 greet("Welcome") } fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 { return a + b } fn greet(name: string) { print(name) } fn main() -> void { let total = add(10, 32) print(total) // 42 greet("Welcome") } fn — starts a function definition. add — the function name. (a: i32, b: i32) — parameters : names and types the caller must provide. -> i32 — return type (what the function gives back). Omit or use -> void when there is no return value. return — sends a value back to the caller. Generics (functions that work on many types) are covered in Generics . Repo example: examples/syntax/math.ny

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Loops — repeat work
docs.body: Loops — repeat work Loops run the same block multiple times. You almost always need let mut for a counter variable.

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: while — repeat while a condition is true
docs.body: while — repeat while a condition is true Without types With types let mut i = 0 while i < 5 { print(i) i = i + 1 } let mut i: i32 = 0 while i < 5 { print(i) i = i + 1 } Each time through the loop, i increases by 1 until i is no longer less than 5.

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: for — iterate a numeric range
docs.body: for — iterate a numeric range for x in start..end runs with x equal to each integer from start up to, but not including, end . Without types With types for i in 0..5 { print(i) // prints 0, then 1, 2, 3, 4 } for i in 0..5 { print(i) // prints 0, then 1, 2, 3, 4 }

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Example: sum numbers with while
docs.body: Example: sum numbers with while Without types With types fn main() { mut sum = 0 mut i = 0 let n = 10 while i < n { sum = sum + i i = i + 1 } print(sum) // 0+1+2+...+9 = 45 } fn main() -> void { mut sum = 0 mut i = 0 let n: i32 = 10 while i < n { sum = sum + i i = i + 1 } print(sum) // 0+1+2+...+9 = 45 } Run: nyra run examples/comparison/loop/sum_loop.ny (uses a larger n ).

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Conditionals — choose what runs
docs.body: Conditionals — choose what runs if runs one block when a condition is true, optionally another when it is false. Without types With types let score = 75 if score >= 60 { print("pass") } else { print("fail") } let grade = if score >= 90 { "A" } else { "B" } let score: i32 = 75 if score >= 60 { print("pass") } else { print("fail") } let grade = if score >= 90 { "A" } else { "B" } The last line is an if expression : it produces a value ( "A" or "B" ) that you bind with let .

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Structs — group related data
docs.body: Structs — group related data A struct groups named fields. In zero-types style, use an object literal directly — no struct keyword. Add a named struct when you need methods ( impl ), imports, or FFI. Without types With types fn main() { let mut p = { hp: 100, score: 0 } p = { hp: p.hp, score: p.score + 10 } print(p.score) // 10 } fn main() -> void { let mut p = { hp: 100, score: 0 } p = { hp: p.hp, score: p.score + 10 } print(p.score) // 10 } Access fields with a dot: p.score , p.hp . Separate literal fields with commas. See Nyra Structs for object literals vs named struct . Repo examples: examples/comparison/struct_sum/struct_sum.ny · examples/projects/calculator/

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Enums & match — fixed choices
docs.body: Enums & match — fixed choices An enum lists allowed variants. match picks code based on which variant you have. Without types With types enum Status { Idle Running Done } fn code(s) { return match s { Status.Idle => 0 Status.Running => 1 Status.Done => 2 } } enum Status { Idle Running Done } fn code(s: Status) -> i32 { return match s { Status.Idle => 0 Status.Running => 1 Status.Done => 2 } } See Match for guards and more patterns. Repo example: examples/language_features/demo.ny

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Imports — split code across files
docs.body: Imports — split code across files Large programs use multiple .ny files. import brings names from another file into the current one. Without types With types import "src/config.ny" import "src/engine.ny" fn main() { print(APP_TITLE) print(run_sim()) } import "src/config.ny" import "src/engine.ny" fn main() -> void { print(APP_TITLE) print(run_sim()) } Paths are relative to the file that contains the import . Run multi-file projects with nyra run . from the folder that contains main.ny . Guides: Imports · Modules · Built-in methods

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Methods — functions on a type
docs.body: Methods — functions on a type Without types With types struct Counter { value: i32 } impl Counter { fn bump(self) { return Counter { value: self.value + 1 } } } fn main() { let c = Counter { value: 0 } let c2 = c.bump() print(c2.value) // 1 } struct Counter { value: i32 } impl Counter { fn bump(self) -> Counter { return Counter { value: self.value + 1 } } } fn main() -> void { let c: Counter = Counter { value: 0 } let c2 = c.bump() print(c2.value) // 1 } impl Counter { ... } attaches methods to Counter . Call them with dot syntax: c.bump() .

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Built-in I/O — output to the screen
docs.body: Built-in I/O — output to the screen print("Hello") // text print(42) // integer print(1 + 2) // any expression time_start("work") // optional: measure how long code takes // ... your code ... time_end("work") More APIs: Standard library .

docs.title: Language basics
docs.heading: Next steps
docs.body: Next steps Getting started — install and first project Syntax cheat sheet — compact keyword list Memory & ownership — Copy, Move, borrow (read after basics) Built-in methods — every builtin with a usage example Learning path — full curriculum Syntax cheat sheet →

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Language syntax
docs.body: Language syntax Core syntax for Nyra v1.2.x — indentation-friendly blocks, minimal keywords. New to Nyra? Start with Language basics . See also Language reference and Standard library .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Hello World
docs.body: Hello World Without types With types fn main() { print("Hello, world!") } fn main() -> void { print("Hello, world!") }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Variables
docs.body: Variables New to programming? Read the full walkthrough: Language basics → Variables .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: let — immutable binding
docs.body: let — immutable binding Creates a name for a value. After initialization, you must not assign a new value to that name. Without types With types let x = 42 // x stays 42 unless you used let mut let x: i32 = 42

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: mut — mutable (changeable)
docs.body: mut — mutable (changeable) mut is short for mutable . Use let mut when the value will change (counters, loops). Without types With types let mut n = 0 // n can be reassigned n = n + 1 let mut n: i32 = 0 n = n + 1

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: const — compile-time constant
docs.body: const — compile-time constant Without types With types const MAX = 100 // fixed name; value known when compiling const MAX = 100 // fixed name; value known when compiling let let mut const Reassign? No Yes No When set? At runtime in code At runtime; can change At compile time string and heap-owned values are Move types — see Memory & ownership .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Functions
docs.body: Functions Without types With types fn add(a, b) { return a + b } fn greet(name) { print(name) } fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 { return a + b } fn greet(name: string) { print(name) } Generic syntax is supported with monomorphization ( fn id<T>(x: T) ).

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Template strings (v1.2)
docs.body: Template strings (v1.2) Without types With types let name = "Nyra" let score = 42 print(`Hello, ${name}!`) print(`score) let name: string = "Nyra" let score: i32 = 42 print(`Hello, ${name}!`) print(`score)

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Arrow functions (v1.2 — Extended)
docs.body: Arrow functions (v1.2 — Extended) Without types With types let add_one = (x) => x + 1 print(add_one(41)) let add_one = (x) => x + 1 print(add_one(41)) Emits warning[W001] unless you use --deny-extended in CI. Useful for HTTP handler slots — see net/http .

docs.body: if / else if x > 0 { print("positive") } else { print("non-positive") }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: while
docs.body: while Without types With types let mut i = 0 while i < 10 { print(i) i = i + 1 } let mut i: i32 = 0 while i < 10 { print(i) i = i + 1 }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: break / continue
docs.body: break / continue break exits the nearest loop; continue skips to the next iteration ( while and range for ). Without types With types let mut sum = 0 let mut i = 0 while i < 10 { i = i + 1 if i == 5 { continue } sum = sum + i } let mut sum: i32 = 0 let mut i: i32 = 0 while i < 10 { i = i + 1 if i == 5 { continue } sum = sum + i }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: for range
docs.body: for range Without types With types for i in 0..5 { print(i) } for i in 0..5 { print(i) }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: if expressions
docs.body: if expressions Without types With types let sign = if x >= 0 { 1 } else { -1 } let sign = if x >= 0 { 1 } else { -1 }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: match
docs.body: match Exhaustive-style matching on enums and values. Supports guards — see Match . Without types With types let n = match color { Color.Red if x > 0 => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 } let n = match color { Color.Red if x > 0 => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 }

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Methods
docs.body: Methods Defined with impl ; desugared to Type_method functions: Without types With types impl Calculator { fn add(self, n) { Calculator { value: self.value + n } } } impl Calculator { fn add(self, n: i32) { Calculator { value: self.value + n } } } Without types With types let c2 = c.add(10) let c2 = c.add(10)

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: I/O
docs.body: I/O Without types With types print("line") // stdout + newline print(42) // integers write("no newline") // buffered println("line") // buffered + newline flush() // flush buffer let s = input("Name? ") print("line") // stdout + newline print(42) // integers write("no newline") // buffered println("line") // buffered + newline flush() // flush buffer let s: string = input("Name? ")

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Colored output
docs.body: Colored output Add an optional color: argument to print . Works in ANSI-capable terminals (macOS Terminal, iTerm, Windows Terminal). print("Error!", color: red) print("OK", color: green) print("Custom", color: "#FF5733") // #RGB or #RRGGBB print("RGB", color: "rgb(0,255,128)") print(msg, color: myColor) // string variable Named colors: red , green , blue , yellow , cyan , magenta , bold , dim , bright_red , … See I/O built-ins .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: String escapes
docs.body: String escapes Double-quoted strings support \n , \t , \\ , \" , octal ( \033 ), hex ( \x1b ), and Unicode ( \u{1b} ).

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: Date & time
docs.body: Date & time Without types With types let d = date() print(d.year) print(d.month) // 1–12 print(d.day) print(d.hour) print(d.minute) print(d.second) print(d.week) // 0=Sunday … 6=Saturday let d: Date = date() print(d.year) print(d.month) // 1–12 print(d.day) print(d.hour) print(d.minute) print(d.second) print(d.week) // 0=Sunday … 6=Saturday Aliases: d.minutes , d.seconds , d.weekday . Full reference: Built-ins → date() .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: String & array built-ins
docs.body: String & array built-ins Without types With types let parts = "a,b,c".split(",") for p in parts { print(p) } let arr = [10, 20, 30] for n in arr { print(n) } print(arr.length()) let parts: VecStr = "a,b,c".split(",") for p in parts { print(p) } let arr: [i32; 3] = [10, 20, 30] for n in arr { print(n) } print(arr.length()) String methods: .trim() , .contains() , .replace() , .to_upper() , … — full list .

docs.title: Language syntax
docs.heading: defer (Extended — prefer Drop)
docs.body: defer (Extended — prefer Drop) Run a call expression when the block ends (LIFO). Extended tier — emits warning[W001] . For memory, auto-drop already frees at } ; for handles, use impl Drop RAII instead of defer free . Without types With types extern fn close_fd(fd){ let fd = open(path) defer close_fd(fd) // OK: FFI teardown; prefer impl Drop for reusable types read_into(fd) } // owned string locals: auto-dropped — no defer needed extern fn close_fd(fd){ let fd = open(path) defer close_fd(fd) // OK: FFI teardown; prefer impl Drop for reusable types read_into(fd) } // owned string locals: auto-dropped — no defer needed Details: Memory → defer vs Drop · nyra-skill.md

docs.title: Nyra Borrowing
docs.heading: Shared read-only borrow
docs.body: Shared read-only borrow Without types With types fn main() { let msg = "hello" let r = &msg print(*r) print(msg) } fn main() -> void { let msg: string = "hello" let r = &msg print(*r) print(msg) } Borrows expire at the last use (non-lexical lifetimes). Rules: Many &T OR one &mut T at a time Cannot use a moved value after move Cannot return &local ← Nyra Ownership Learn overview Nyra Data Structures →

docs.title: Nyra Data Structures
docs.heading: Quick tour
docs.body: Quick tour Without types With types import "stdlib/map" fn main() { let nums = [1, 2, 3] print(nums.len()) let pair = (42, "ok") print(pair.0) let mut scores = HashMap_str_i32_new() scores = scores.insert("ada", 100) print(scores.get("ada")) } import "stdlib/map" fn main() -> void { let nums: [i32; 3] = [1, 2, 3] print(nums.len()) let pair = (42, "ok") print(pair.0) let mut scores = HashMap_str_i32_new() scores = scores.insert("ada", 100) print(scores.get("ada")) } Try it nyra run examples/syntax/hashmap.ny Expected output: 100 true HashMap runnable demo; combine with array and tuple snippets from sibling lessons. ← Nyra Borrowing Learn overview Nyra Arrays →

docs.title: Nyra Data Types
docs.heading: Nyra Data Types
docs.body: Nyra Data Types Built-in types: integers, booleans, strings, and more. Type Example Notes i32 42 32-bit signed integer (default for literals) i64 10000000000 64-bit signed integer u32 42 32-bit unsigned integer bool true , false Boolean string "hello" UTF-8 text (heap-owned when dynamic) void — No return value

docs.title: Nyra HashMap
docs.heading: Nyra HashMap
docs.body: Nyra HashMap Key-value maps: runtime naming map_ _ _ and stdlib wrappers.

docs.title: Nyra HashMap
docs.heading: Runtime naming: map_<key>_<value>_<op>
docs.body: Runtime naming: map_<key>_<value>_<op> Every hash-map C runtime symbol follows one pattern: map_<key_type>_<value_type>_<operation> Key type — first segment after map_ (e.g. str , i32 ) Value type — second segment (e.g. i32 , str ) Operation — last segment: new , insert , get , contains , remove , keys , free , retain Examples: map_str_i32_insert , map_str_str_get , map_i32_i32_contains . When key and value share the same type (like Go map[int]int ), both appear in the name — map_i32_i32_* is intentional so the ABI stays explicit. See the full symbol list in Runtime bindings .

docs.title: Nyra HashMap
docs.heading: String keys, i32 values — map_str_i32_*
docs.body: String keys, i32 values — map_str_i32_* Without types With types extern fn map_str_i32_new() -> ptr extern fn map_str_i32_insert(m: ptr, key: string, value: i32) -> void extern fn map_str_i32_get(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_i32_contains(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_i32_free(m: ptr) -> void fn main() { let m = map_str_i32_new() map_str_i32_insert(m, "score", 100) print(map_str_i32_get(m, "score")) print(map_str_i32_contains(m, "score")) map_str_i32_free(m) } extern fn map_str_i32_new() -> ptr extern fn map_str_i32_insert(m: ptr, key: string, value: i32) -> void extern fn map_str_i32_get(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_i32_contains(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_i32_free(m: ptr) -> void fn main() -> void { let m = map_str_i32_new() map_str_i32_insert(m, "score", 100) print(map_str_i32_get(m, "score")) print(map_str_i32_contains(m, "score")) map_str_i32_free(m) }

docs.title: Nyra HashMap
docs.heading: String keys, string values — map_str_str_*
docs.body: String keys, string values — map_str_str_* Without types With types extern fn map_str_str_new() -> ptr extern fn map_str_str_insert(m: ptr, key: string, value: string) -> void extern fn map_str_str_get(m: ptr, key: string) -> string extern fn map_str_str_contains(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_str_free(m: ptr) -> void fn main() { let m = map_str_str_new() map_str_str_insert(m, "lang", "Nyra") print(map_str_str_get(m, "lang")) map_str_str_free(m) } extern fn map_str_str_new() -> ptr extern fn map_str_str_insert(m: ptr, key: string, value: string) -> void extern fn map_str_str_get(m: ptr, key: string) -> string extern fn map_str_str_contains(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_str_free(m: ptr) -> void fn main() -> void { let m = map_str_str_new() map_str_str_insert(m, "lang", "Nyra") print(map_str_str_get(m, "lang")) map_str_str_free(m) }

docs.title: Nyra HashMap
docs.heading: Integer keys and values — map_i32_i32_*
docs.body: Integer keys and values — map_i32_i32_* Without types With types extern fn map_i32_i32_new() -> ptr extern fn map_i32_i32_insert(m: ptr, key: i32, value: i32) -> void extern fn map_i32_i32_get(m: ptr, key: i32) -> i32 extern fn map_i32_i32_contains(m: ptr, key: i32) -> i32 extern fn map_i32_i32_free(m: ptr) -> void fn main() { let m = map_i32_i32_new() map_i32_i32_insert(m, 42, 99) print(map_i32_i32_get(m, 42)) print(map_i32_i32_contains(m, 42)) map_i32_i32_free(m) } extern fn map_i32_i32_new() -> ptr extern fn map_i32_i32_insert(m: ptr, key: i32, value: i32) -> void extern fn map_i32_i32_get(m: ptr, key: i32) -> i32 extern fn map_i32_i32_contains(m: ptr, key: i32) -> i32 extern fn map_i32_i32_free(m: ptr) -> void fn main() -> void { let m = map_i32_i32_new() map_i32_i32_insert(m, 42, 99) print(map_i32_i32_get(m, 42)) print(map_i32_i32_contains(m, 42)) map_i32_i32_free(m) }

docs.title: Nyra HashMap
docs.heading: Stdlib wrappers (recommended)
docs.body: Stdlib wrappers (recommended) Import stdlib/map.ny for HashMap_str_i32 and HashMap_str_str — method syntax and automatic cleanup via Drop . The low-level map_* names stay stable for extern fn and FFI. Without types With types import "stdlib/map" fn main() { let mut scores = HashMap_str_i32_new() scores = scores.insert("ada", 100) print(scores.get("ada")) } import "stdlib/map" fn main() -> void { let mut scores = HashMap_str_i32_new() scores = scores.insert("ada", 100) print(scores.get("ada")) }

docs.title: Nyra HashMap
docs.heading: Runnable example
docs.body: Runnable example Without types With types extern fn map_str_i32_new() -> ptr extern fn map_str_i32_insert(m: ptr, key: string, value: i32) -> void extern fn map_str_i32_get(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_i32_contains(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_i32_free(m: ptr) -> void fn main() { let handle = map_str_i32_new() map_str_i32_insert(handle, "score", 100) print(map_str_i32_get(handle, "score")) print(map_str_i32_contains(handle, "score")) map_str_i32_free(handle) } extern fn map_str_i32_new() -> ptr extern fn map_str_i32_insert(m: ptr, key: string, value: i32) -> void extern fn map_str_i32_get(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_i32_contains(m: ptr, key: string) -> i32 extern fn map_str_i32_free(m: ptr) -> void fn main() -> void { let handle = map_str_i32_new() map_str_i32_insert(handle, "score", 100) print(map_str_i32_get(handle, "score")) print(map_str_i32_contains(handle, "score")) map_str_i32_free(handle) } Try it nyra run examples/syntax/hashmap.ny Expected output: 100 true ← Nyra Tuples Learn overview Nyra Structs →

docs.title: Nyra Intro
docs.heading: Nyra Intro
docs.body: Nyra Intro What Nyra is, why it exists, and how this tutorial is organized. Nyra is a fast, memory-safe programming language with Rust-inspired ownership, a minimal syntax, and LLVM-backed native compilation.

docs.title: Nyra Intro
docs.heading: Your first program
docs.body: Your first program Without types With types fn main() { print("Hello, Nyra!") } fn main() -> void { print("Hello, Nyra!") } Try it nyra run examples/syntax/hello.ny Expected output: Hello, Nyra!

docs.title: Nyra Intro
docs.heading: What you will learn
docs.body: What you will learn This tutorial follows a W3Schools-style path: one topic per page, runnable examples, and prev/next navigation. Start with Get Started or jump to any sidebar topic. Basics — variables, types, control flow, functions, strings Memory — ownership and borrowing (Nyra's safety model) Data structures — arrays, vectors, tuples, hash maps, structs, enums Prerequisites: Install Nyra and clang . Learn overview Nyra Get Started →

docs.title: Nyra Loops
docs.heading: Nyra Loops
docs.body: Nyra Loops Overview of while and for loops. Nyra has two loop forms: while condition { ... } — repeat while true for i in start..end { ... } — iterate a half-open range Details: While loops · For loops

docs.body: Sum with for Without types With types fn main() { let mut sum = 0 for i in 0..5 { sum = sum + i } print(sum) } fn main() -> void { let mut sum: i32 = 0 for i in 0..5 { sum = sum + i } print(sum) } ← Nyra Match Learn overview Nyra While Loops →

docs.title: Nyra Match
docs.heading: match
docs.body: match Without types With types enum Color { Red Green Blue } fn main() { let c = Color.Red let n = match c { Color.Red => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 } print(n) } enum Color { Red Green Blue } fn main() -> void { let c = Color.Red let n = match c { Color.Red => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 } print(n) } Guards: Color.Red if x > 0 => 1 . Wildcard: _ => 0 . See Match reference . Try it nyra run examples/language_features/demo.ny Expected output: 1 ← Nyra If..Else Learn overview Nyra Loops →

docs.title: Nyra Ownership
docs.heading: Nyra Ownership
docs.body: Nyra Ownership Copy vs Move — how Nyra tracks who owns heap data. Type Kind Behavior i32 , bool , enums Copy Both bindings stay valid after assign string (heap) Move Single owner; source invalidated on move struct Copy or Move Move if any field is Move

docs.title: Nyra Ownership
docs.heading: Move example
docs.body: Move example Without types With types fn main() { let a = "hello" let b = a // print(a) // error: use of moved value print(b) } fn main() -> void { let a: string = "hello" let b = a // print(a) // error: use of moved value print(b) } Owned heap strings are auto-dropped at scope end. Deep dive: Memory & ownership . ← Nyra Strings Learn overview Nyra Borrowing →

docs.title: Nyra Scope
docs.heading: Nyra Scope
docs.body: Nyra Scope Where variables are visible — block scope in Nyra.

docs.title: Nyra Scope
docs.heading: Block scope
docs.body: Block scope Without types With types fn main() { let x = 1 if true { let y = 2 print(x) // ok — outer scope print(y) } // print(y) // error — y not in scope } fn main() -> void { let x: i32 = 1 if true { let y: i32 = 2 print(x) // ok — outer scope print(y) } // print(y) // error — y not in scope } Each { } block creates a new scope. Bindings declared inside a block are not visible outside it. ← Nyra Functions Learn overview Nyra Strings →

Use let mut when the value must change. Shorthand inside functions: mut x = 0 . docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Match expressions
docs.body: Match expressions Exhaustiveness, guards, and tag-only enums. Without types With types let n = match color { Color.Red => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 } let n = match color { Color.Red => 1 Color.Green => 2 Color.Blue => 3 }

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Nested enum binds
docs.body: Nested enum binds Peel a payload enum inside a variant arm (v1.33+). Shorthand infers the inner enum from the payload type: Without types With types enum Option_i32 { None, Some(i32) } enum Result_Opt { Ok(Option_i32), Fail(Option_i32) } match r { Result_Opt.Ok(Some(x)) => x Result_Opt.Ok(Option_i32.None) => 0 Result_Opt.Fail(_) => -1 } enum Option_i32 { None, Some(i32) } enum Result_Opt { Ok(Option_i32), Fail(Option_i32) } match r { Result_Opt.Ok(Some(x)) => x Result_Opt.Ok(Option_i32.None) => 0 Result_Opt.Fail(_) => -1 } Enum variants with different payload types in one enum (e.g. Ok(Option) + Err(i32) ) are not supported yet.

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Struct and tuple patterns
docs.body: Struct and tuple patterns Since v1.37.0 , destructure structs and tuples in match arms. Field shorthand binds the field name; use _ to ignore a slot. Without types With types match p { Point { x, y } => x + y } match pair { (a, b) => a + b } match p { Point { x, y } => x + y } match pair { (a, b) => a + b } Field-value patterns (e.g. Point { x: 0, y } ) are not supported yet — patterns bind fields only.

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Or-patterns
docs.body: Or-patterns Combine patterns that share the same body with | (single pipe, not || ): Without types With types match c { Color.Red | Color.Blue => 1 Color.Green => 2 } match method { "GET" | "HEAD" => 200 "POST" | "PUT" => 201 _ => 400 } match c { Color.Red | Color.Blue => 1 Color.Green => 2 } match method { "GET" | "HEAD" => 200 "POST" | "PUT" => 201 _ => 400 }

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Guards
docs.body: Guards Without types With types match r { Result.Ok(v) if v > 0 => v Result.Ok(_v) => 0 Result.Err(e) => e } match r { Result.Ok(v) if v > 0 => v Result.Ok(_v) => 0 Result.Err(e) => e }

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: Payload enums
docs.body: Payload enums Import stdlib/option.ny for generic Option<T> / Result<T,E> , or declare a monomorph enum: Without types With types enum Option_i32 { None, Some(i32) } let x = Option_i32.Some(42) let n = match x { Option_i32.Some(v) => v, Option_i32.None => 0, } enum Option_i32 { None, Some(i32) } let x = Option_i32.Some(42) let n = match x { Option_i32.Some(v) => v, Option_i32.None => 0, } match on a parameter typed Option<T> or Result<T,E> lowers the same as on a local binding. Result.Ok(7) at a call site is monomorphized from the callee parameter type (no extra let annotation needed).

docs.title: Match expressions
docs.heading: String matching
docs.body: String matching Since Nyra v1.17.0 , match works on string scrutinees with string literal arms (compared via strcmp ). Use _ for the default arm. Without types With types fn http_status(method) { return match method { "GET" => 200, "POST" => 201, _ => 404, } } fn http_status(method: i32) -> i32 { return match method { "GET" => 200, "POST" => 201, _ => 404, } } Arm bodies may use a trailing comma. Built-in tag-only Option / Result (no import) support ?? / ?. only — not stored payloads. Syntax →

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Memory & ownership
docs.body: Memory & ownership How Nyra manages memory at compile time — no garbage collector, no manual free in normal code, and no leaks when you follow the rules the compiler enforces. For developer-friendly ownership (auto-borrow, auto-Copy, inference) see Ownership UX . On this page Philosophy: safety without a GC Ownership UX (v2.8+) Copy vs Move types Ownership rules Automatic cleanup (Drop plan) Non-lexical borrows (NLL) Lifetimes How Nyra prevents memory leaks Double-free & use-after-free defer (LIFO cleanup) Custom Drop for structs spawn, closures & Send/Sync FFI boundary Compiler pipeline Common errors & fixes Examples cookbook

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Philosophy: safety without a GC
docs.body: Philosophy: safety without a GC Nyra uses compile-time ownership inspired by Rust, with Go-like syntax. Every value has a clear owner. When ownership ends, the compiler inserts cleanup code — you never call free on ordinary strings or owned return values. No garbage collector — deterministic, low-latency native code. No reference counting in the language core — ownership is static. Zero cost for i32 , bool , enums without payloads — stack only. Compile errors instead of runtime crashes for use-after-move, double-free, and dangling references. Escape analysis — Nyra proves local values at compile time and promotes them to the stack; sequential channels can become lock-free LocalChannel . See Escape analysis .

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Ownership UX (v2.8–v3.1)
docs.body: Ownership UX (v2.8–v3.1) Nyra keeps Rust’s static ownership model but reduces day-to-day friction: Auto-borrow — save(user) becomes save(&user) when the callee expects &User . Auto-Copy — structs like Point { x: i32 y: i32 } copy on assign without #[derive(Copy)] . Inference — return types, generic call sites ( id(7) ), and let price = 3.14 → f64 . Explicit control — move user / clone user at calls; Swift-style errors with fix-it notes. Full guide: Ownership UX · Conformance: CONF-INF-* , CONF-COERCE-* , CONF-DIAG-* , CONF-COPY-* , CONF-F64-* . Without types With types struct User { name: string age: i32 } fn save(u: &User) { print(u.name) } fn main() { let user = User { name: "Ada", age: 30 } save(user) print(user.name) } struct User { name: string age: i32 } fn save(u: &User) { print(u.name) } fn main() -> void { let user: User = User { name: "Ada", age: 30 } save(user) print(user.name) }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Copy vs Move types
docs.body: Copy vs Move types The compiler classifies every type as Copy or Move ( ownership/kind.rs ). Kind Types On assignment / pass At scope end Copy i32 , i64 , u32 , f64 , bool , enum tags, ptr , fn pointers, all-Copy structs Bitwise copy — both names valid Stack discarded — no heap call Move string (heap C-string), structs with move fields, types with impl Drop Ownership transfers — old name invalid Auto free or custom Drop_*_drop Without types With types fn main() { let a = 10 let b = a // Copy — both usable print(a) print(b) let s = "hello" let t = s // Move — s is now invalid print(t) // print(s) // ERROR: use of moved value 's' } fn main() -> void { let a: i32 = 10 let b = a // Copy — both usable print(a) print(b) let s: string = "hello" let t = s // Move — s is now invalid print(t) // print(s) // ERROR: use of moved value 's' }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Ownership rules
docs.body: Ownership rules One owner — each heap-owned value has exactly one binding responsible for cleanup. Move by default — passing a string to a function or assigning it moves ownership unless the binding is mut and the callee borrows. Borrow, don’t steal — &T and &mut T temporarily share access without transferring ownership. No active borrows on move — you cannot move a value while an immutable or mutable reference to it is live (NLL tracks this). Returned owned values — functions like read_file , strcat return new heap string s; the caller owns the result. Without types With types fn greet(name) { return strcat("Hello, ", name) } fn main() { let msg = greet("Nyra") print(msg) } fn greet(name: string) -> i32 { return strcat("Hello, ", name) } fn main() -> void { let msg = greet("Nyra") print(msg) }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Automatic cleanup (Drop plan)
docs.body: Automatic cleanup (Drop plan) After type-checking, the ownership analyzer builds a DropPlan per function ( ownership/drop.rs ): Scan every let binding and infer whether it owns heap memory. Track moves — if a value is moved, mark it so it is not dropped twice. At each scope exit (end of block, before return , on branch merge), codegen emits emit_auto_drops . For string locals still owned → call free(ptr) . For structs with impl Drop → call Drop_TypeName_drop . Without types With types fn load_and_print() { let data = read_file("config.txt") // owned heap string print(data) } // compiler inserts: free(data) fn load_and_print() { let data = read_file("config.txt") // owned heap string print(data) } // compiler inserts: free(data) You do not write free(data) in normal Nyra code. The compiler guarantees cleanup on every path that still owns the binding.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Composite struct cleanup (v2.3)
docs.body: Composite struct cleanup (v2.3) Structs with heap-owned fields (e.g. string ) but no custom impl Drop get automatic field-wise cleanup — the compiler emits free for each owned field at scope end: Without types With types fn main() { let p = { id: 1, body: read_file("data.txt") } print(p.id) } // free(p.body) inserted automatically fn main() -> void { let p = { id: 1, body: read_file("data.txt") } print(p.id) } // free(p.body) inserted automatically Nested structs without custom Drop are dropped recursively. Custom impl Drop still takes full control (no double field-free).

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Owned extern fn returns (v2.3)
docs.body: Owned extern fn returns (v2.3) Any extern fn foo(...) -> string in your program is automatically treated as returning heap-owned memory — no need to register symbols in a compiler whitelist: Without types With types extern fn my_api_load(){ let s = my_api_load() // auto-owned, auto-dropped print(s) } extern fn my_api_load(){ let s = my_api_load() // auto-owned, auto-dropped print(s) }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Partial field moves (v2.4)
docs.body: Partial field moves (v2.4) Moving a single field out of a struct invalidates the parent binding (Rust-style), preventing use-after-partial-move bugs: Without types With types fn main() { let p = { a: 1, body: "hello" } let body = p.body // move field — p is now invalid print(body) // print(p.a) // ERROR: use of moved value 'p' } fn main() -> void { let p = { a: 1, body: "hello" } let body = p.body // move field — p is now invalid print(body) // print(p.a) // ERROR: use of moved value 'p' }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Generic Option / Result (v2.4)
docs.body: Generic Option / Result (v2.4) Without types With types enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) } let msg = Option.Some("hello") // monomorph → Option__string let n = Option.Some(42) // literal → Option__i32 enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) } let msg = Option.Some("hello") // monomorph → Option__string let n = Option.Some(42) // literal → Option__i32 Heap payloads in generic enums (e.g. Option<string> ) get automatic payload cleanup at scope end. Import from stdlib: import "stdlib/option.ny" . Typed annotations and match patterns rewrite to monomorphized names ( Option__string ) at compile time.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Monolith-friendly structs + strings (v2.4)
docs.body: Monolith-friendly structs + strings (v2.4) Large single-file apps can combine multi-field structs, partial field moves, generic Option<string> , and Box<T> without manual Drop for string fields: Without types With types import "stdlib/option.ny" import "stdlib/box.ny" struct UserRecord Send { id: i32, name: string, email: string } fn lookup(id, label) { if id > 0 { return Option.Some(label) } return Option.None } fn main() { let u = UserRecord { id: 1, name: "Ada", email: "ada@nyra.dev" } let id = u.id let name = u.name let opt = Option.Some(name) match opt { Option.Some(n) => print(n), Option.None => print("none"), } let boxed = Box<string> { value: "cache" } print(id) print(boxed.value) } import "stdlib/option.ny" import "stdlib/box.ny" struct UserRecord Send { id: i32, name: string, email: string } fn lookup(id: i32, label: i32) -> i32 { if id > 0 { return Option.Some(label) } return Option.None } fn main() -> void { let u: UserRecord = UserRecord { id: 1, name: "Ada", email: "ada@nyra.dev" } let id = u.id let name = u.name let opt = Option.Some(name) match opt { Option.Some(n) => print(n), Option.None => print("none"), } let boxed = Box<string> { value: "cache" } print(id) print(boxed.value) } See examples/monolith_struct_smoke.ny for a full compile-ready sample.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Shared ownership with Arc<T> (v2.5)
docs.body: Shared ownership with Arc<T> (v2.5) Reference-counted sharing for graphs and cross-scope labels. Import stdlib/arc.ny : Without types With types import "stdlib/arc.ny" struct GraphEdge Send { from_id: i32 to_id: i32 shared_label: Arc<string> } fn share_label(name) { let root = Arc_from_string(name) return Arc_clone_string(root) } fn main() { let shared = share_label("node-alpha") let edge = GraphEdge { from_id: 1, to_id: 2, shared_label: shared } print(Arc_get_string(edge.shared_label)) } import "stdlib/arc.ny" struct GraphEdge Send { from_id: i32 to_id: i32 shared_label: Arc<string> } fn share_label(name: string) -> i32 { let root = Arc_from_string(name) return Arc_clone_string(root) } fn main() -> void { let shared = share_label("node-alpha") let edge: GraphEdge = GraphEdge { from_id: 1, to_id: 2, shared_label: shared } print(Arc_get_string(edge.shared_label)) } Arc<T> is Send + Sync ; Arc_clone_string / Arc_clone_i32 increment refcount; last Drop frees the payload ( arc_dec_i32 / arc_dec_string ). See examples/graph_arc_smoke.ny .

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: When auto-drop runs
docs.body: When auto-drop runs Event What gets dropped End of fn / block All owned bindings still in scope and not moved return Owned locals not returned as the value Move to function arg Source binding — callee or copy owns cleanup defer expr Deferred expressions run LIFO before scope exit

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Non-lexical borrows (NLL)
docs.body: Non-lexical borrows (NLL) Unlike lexical-only systems, Nyra ends borrows at the last use of the reference, not at the closing brace. This is Non-Lexical Lifetimes ( ownership/nll.rs → compute_last_uses ). Without types With types fn main() { let mut s = "hello" let r = &s // immutable borrow starts print(r) // last use of r — borrow ENDS here s = "world" // OK — no active borrow } fn main() -> void { let mut s = "hello" let r = &s // immutable borrow starts print(r) // last use of r — borrow ENDS here s = "world" // OK — no active borrow } Without types With types fn broken() { let mut s = "hello" let r = &mut s // mutable borrow print(s) // ERROR: cannot use s while mutably borrowed print(r) } fn broken() { let mut s = "hello" let r = &mut s // mutable borrow print(s) // ERROR: cannot use s while mutably borrowed print(r) } See also: Nyra Borrowing for a guided lesson.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Lifetimes
docs.body: Lifetimes References carry lifetimes so the compiler can reject dangling references (returning &local after the local is destroyed). Without types With types // ERROR: cannot return reference to local fn dangling(){ let s = "tmp" return &s } // OK: return value transfers ownership instead fn owned() { let s = "tmp" return s } // ERROR: cannot return reference to local fn dangling(){ let s: string = "tmp" return &s } // OK: return value transfers ownership instead fn owned() -> i32 { let s: string = "tmp" return s } Explicit: fn pick<'a>(x: &'a string) -> &'a string Elision: one input reference → output shares that lifetime HRTB: for<'a> fn(&'a string) -> &'a string for higher-order callbacks

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: How Nyra prevents memory leaks
docs.body: How Nyra prevents memory leaks A memory leak is heap memory that is never freed. Nyra prevents leaks in normal code through:

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 1. Drop plan covers every owned binding
docs.body: 1. Drop plan covers every owned binding If you write let s = read_file(...) and never move s , the compiler always emits free at scope end — even if you forget the variable exists.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 2. Moves transfer cleanup responsibility
docs.body: 2. Moves transfer cleanup responsibility Without types With types fn consume(s) { print(s) } // consume's drop plan frees s here fn main() { let path = "data.txt" let content = read_file(path) consume(content) // ownership moved to consume — main won't double-free } fn consume(s: string) { print(s) } // consume's drop plan frees s here fn main() -> void { let path: string = "data.txt" let content = read_file(path) consume(content) // ownership moved to consume — main won't double-free }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 3. No silent heap allocation without ownership
docs.body: 3. No silent heap allocation without ownership Functions that return heap memory ( strcat , read_file , sys_recv , …) are registered in OWNED_EXTERN_RETURNS . The caller must bind the result — orphaning is impossible because unused expressions in let still get a binding or the value is moved/consumed.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 4. Closure & spawn heap env freed at scope end
docs.body: 4. Closure & spawn heap env freed at scope end Escaping closures (v2.2) that malloc capture envs register heap_owned metadata — freed when the let binding ends. Stack closures need no heap free.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: 5. Custom Drop for resource types
docs.body: 5. Custom Drop for resource types Without types With types struct FileHandle { fd: i32 } impl Drop for FileHandle { fn drop(self) { nyra_close(self.fd) // always runs when FileHandle goes out of scope } } struct FileHandle { fd: i32 } impl Drop for FileHandle { fn drop(self) { nyra_close(self.fd) // always runs when FileHandle goes out of scope } }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: What Nyra does not prevent automatically
docs.body: What Nyra does not prevent automatically Intentional leak via FFI — returning a raw ptr to C without documenting ownership. Manual free on live bindings — compiler warns; double-free if you free then auto-drop runs. Cycles — no cycle collector; avoid reference cycles in raw pointers.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Double-free & use-after-free
docs.body: Double-free & use-after-free Nyra blocks these at compile time: Bug Compile-time check Example error Use after move Move tracking in borrowck Use of moved value 's' Double free Moved values skipped in DropPlan Cannot happen if code compiles Free then use free on owned binding → warning + moved mark Manual free warning Dangling & Lifetime checker cannot return reference to local Data race (thread) Send / Sync on spawn captures captured value is not Send Without types With types fn main() { let s = "hello" let t = s // move print(s) // ERROR: use of moved value 's' } fn main() -> void { let s: string = "hello" let t = s // move print(s) // ERROR: use of moved value 's' }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: defer vs Drop (Extended)
docs.body: defer vs Drop (Extended) Name: defer expr Explanation: Schedules a call at scope exit (LIFO). Prefer impl Drop for resources — do not defer free on owned strings. Example: methods gallery · nyra run examples/builtins/defer/defer_return.ny Without types With types allow_extended fn cleanup() { print(1) } fn main() { defer cleanup() return } allow_extended fn cleanup() { print(1) } fn main() -> void { defer cleanup() return } Output: 1 (before function returns). LIFO demo: #ex-defer-lifo → body , a , b . defer schedules a call expression at scope exit (LIFO). It lives in the Extended tier. For most code, auto-drop and impl Drop replace defer — do not use defer free on owned strings (double-free risk). Need Prefer defer ? Heap string at } Auto-drop No FFI handle / file impl Drop wrapper (RAII) Only one-off defer extern_close(h) if you skip a struct Log at scope exit Write before return or in Drop Optional defer log_done() Without types With types extern fn gzclose(f){ handle: ptr } impl Drop for GzFile { fn drop(self) { unsafe { gzclose(self.handle) } } } fn main() { let f = GzFile { handle: open_gz("log.txt.gz") } print(read_all(f.handle)) } // gzclose via Drop — preferred over defer extern fn gzclose(f){ handle: ptr } impl Drop for GzFile { fn drop(self) { unsafe { gzclose(self.handle) } } } fn main() -> void { let f = GzFile { handle: open_gz("log.txt.gz") } print(read_all(f.handle)) } // gzclose via Drop — preferred over defer Why Extended, not Core: Drop covers cleanup semantics Core needs; defer overlaps and is mainly for niche FFI/side-effect hooks. Core-only CI uses nyra check --deny-extended with auto-drop only. See nyra-skill.md § defer vs Drop.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Custom Drop for structs
docs.body: Custom Drop for structs Without types With types struct Buffer { data: ptr, len: i32 } impl Drop for Buffer { fn drop(self) { free(self.data) } } fn main() { let b = Buffer { data: allocate(), len: 1024 } } // Drop_Buffer_drop called automatically struct Buffer { data: ptr, len: i32 } impl Drop for Buffer { fn drop(self) { free(self.data) } } fn main() -> void { let b: Buffer = Buffer { data: allocate(), len: 1024 } } // Drop_Buffer_drop called automatically Structs with impl Drop are always Move types — they cannot be silently copied.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: spawn, closures & Send/Sync
docs.body: spawn, closures & Send/Sync

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: spawn
docs.body: spawn spawn { ... } packs captured variables into a struct, heap-copies it, and starts a pthread . Rules: Copy captures ( i32 , bool ) — copied into the thread struct. Move captures ( string ) — must be Send ; marked moved in the parent. Active & / &mut — compile error.

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Closures
docs.body: Closures Capturing lambdas follow the same rules — no reference captures; move types transfer ownership. See Closures .

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: FFI boundary
docs.body: FFI boundary Inside Nyra: auto-drop. At the C boundary: export fn returning string → caller outside Nyra must call free . extern fn returning string → Nyra caller auto-owns and auto-drops. Do not call free on live Nyra bindings — the compiler warns. Full ABI: FFI & ABI .

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Compiler pipeline
docs.body: Compiler pipeline Source (.ny) → Lexer → Parser → Macro expand (??, ?., struct ctors, move/clone desugar) → Monomorph (+ generic call-site inference) → Auto-borrow coercion → Const fold → Typecheck → Ownership analysis → DropPlan (+ auto-Copy inference) → Borrow check (NLL moves/borrows, callee-aware diagnostics) → Lifetime check → Send / Sync check → LLVM codegen (auto-drops, spawn, closures, f64/double) → opt → clang + nyra_rt.c → binary Inspect without codegen: nyra check . · Structured errors: nyra diag . --json

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Common errors & fixes
docs.body: Common errors & fixes Error Cause Fix Use of moved value 'x' Used after move Borrow with & , use clone x / .clone() , or change callee to &T (auto-borrow applies) Cannot borrow as mutable Two &mut or & + &mut Shrink borrow scope; use NLL — end borrow before second use cannot return reference to local Dangling ref Return owned string or take &'a parameter cannot capture reference in closure & in lambda capture Capture owned copy or use Send move value free on live owned binding Manual free + auto-drop Remove manual free; let auto-drop handle it More: Diagnostics

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Read file — zero manual free
docs.body: Read file — zero manual free Without types With types extern fn read_file(path){ let text = read_file("README.md") print(text) } // free(text) inserted by compiler extern fn read_file(path){ let text = read_file("README.md") print(text) } // free(text) inserted by compiler

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Concatenate strings — both operands moved unless borrowed
docs.body: Concatenate strings — both operands moved unless borrowed Without types With types extern fn strcat(a, b){ let a = "Hello, " let b = "Nyra" let msg = strcat(a, b) // a and b moved into strcat; new string returned print(msg) } // only msg freed — a,b already consumed extern fn strcat(a, b){ let a: string = "Hello, " let b: string = "Nyra" let msg = strcat(a, b) // a and b moved into strcat; new string returned print(msg) } // only msg freed — a,b already consumed

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Safe mutation with &mut
docs.body: Safe mutation with &mut Without types With types fn bump(mut n) { n = n + 1 print(n) } fn main() { let mut count = 0 bump(count) // i32 is Copy — count still valid print(count) } fn bump(mut n: i32) { n = n + 1 print(n) } fn main() -> void { let mut count: i32 = 0 bump(count) // i32 is Copy — count still valid print(count) }

docs.title: Memory & ownership
docs.heading: Option ?? — no leak on either branch
docs.body: Option ?? — no leak on either branch Without types With types import "stdlib/option.ny" fn main() { let x = Option.None let y = x ?? 42 // i32 Copy — no heap print(y) } import "stdlib/option.ny" fn main() -> void { let x = Option.None let y = x ?? 42 // i32 Copy — no heap print(y) } ← Nyra Borrowing Learn overview Concurrency → Language reference →

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: Built-in methods
docs.body: Built-in methods One-page lookup for compiler builtins and stdlib method syntax on strings, numbers, arrays, vectors, maps, and objects. Each row includes a quick example ; full runnable programs are in the gallery below . Quick tip Press Ctrl+K and type a method name (e.g. split , push , abs ). For full module APIs (HTTP, crypto, FS), see Standard library . On this page Overview I/O String methods Number & math Booleans Fixed arrays Vectors & lists Maps (objects) Split lists Timing & memory Other builtins JS-style helpers (import) Runnable examples

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: Overview
docs.body: Overview Category Import? Receiver / type I/O , timing , date , math intrinsics No Global functions String methods No string — borrows receiver Array methods No [T; N] fixed arrays Vec / StrVec stdlib/vec.ny or stdlib/vec_str.ny ptr handles or StrVec struct HashMap stdlib/map.ny HashMap_str_i32 , HashMap_str_str Array_/String_/Math_/JSON_ stdlib/builtins_*.ny Function-style wrappers Runnable gallery: examples/builtins/ · detailed stdlib modules: stdlib → Built-ins

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: I/O no import
docs.body: I/O no import Call Args Returns Notes Example print(expr) value void Stdout + newline print(42) · print("hi") print(expr, color: spec) value + color void ANSI: red , "#RGB" , rgb(r,g,b) print("err", color: red) write(expr) value void Buffered stdout, no newline write("> ") println(expr) value void Buffered stdout + newline println("done") flush() — void Flush stdout buffer write("…"); flush() input() — string Read one stdin line let line = input() input(prompt) string string Print prompt, then read line let name = input("Name? ") Examples: print · input

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: String methods no import
docs.body: String methods no import Method syntax on string — borrows the receiver (does not move). Heap copy: clone s keyword. Method Args Returns Description Example .length() / .len() — i32 Byte length "hello".len() .split(sep) string split list Split on separator "a,b,c".split(",") .trim() — string Strip whitespace " hi ".trim() .contains(needle) string i32 1 if found "abc".contains("b") .starts_with(prefix) string i32 Prefix test "hello".starts_with("he") .ends_with(suffix) string i32 Suffix test "hello".ends_with("lo") .replace(from, to) 2 × string string Replace all "a-b".replace("-", "_") .replacen(from, to, count) 2 × string , i32 string Replace at most count "aaa".replacen("a", "b", 2) .to_upper() — string ASCII upper-case "hi".to_upper() .to_lower() — string ASCII lower-case "HI".to_lower() clone s — string Heap copy (keyword) let copy = clone s Examples: .split() · .trim() · for c in string

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: Number & math no import
docs.body: Number & math no import Compiler math intrinsics — lowered to LLVM, no call overhead. Type overload on abs . Call Args Returns Notes Example abs(x) i32 or f64 same as arg Absolute value abs(-5) abs_i32(x) / abs_f64(x) 1 numeric i32 / f64 Explicit typed variants abs_i32(-42) min_i32(a, b) / max_i32(a, b) 2 × i32 i32 Min / max max_i32(3, 7) min_f64(a, b) / max_f64(a, b) 2 × f64 f64 Min / max min_f64(1.0, 2.0) clamp_i32(x, lo, hi) 3 × i32 i32 Clamp to range clamp_i32(15, 0, 10) cpu_count() — i32 Logical CPU count cpu_count() Example: abs / min / max · See also Math_* helpers

docs.body: Timing & memory no import Call Description Example time_start(label) / time_end(label) Wall-clock timing time_start("work"); …; time_end("work") mem_start(label) / mem_end(label) RSS delta mem_start("alloc"); …; mem_end("alloc") benchmark { ... } Time + memory + CPU% for a block benchmark { heavy_work() } Examples: time_start / time_end · mem_start / mem_end · benchmark { }

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: Other builtins no import
docs.body: Other builtins no import Call Returns / effect Notes Example random() / random(min, max) generic integer ChaCha20 CSPRNG; return type follows bounds ( i32 , i64 , u64 , …). No-arg defaults to i32 . random(1, 6) · random<i64>(50, 100) random_f64() / random_f64(min, max) f64 Unit interval [0, 1) or range [min, max) random_f64(0.0, 1.0) date() Date struct Local clock fields let d = date(); print(d.year) spawn { ... } / spawn:task { ... } JoinHandle (expr) / none (stmt) Lightweight task pool; needs allow_extended ; not on wasm32 spawn { print(1) } spawn:thread { ... } JoinHandle (expr) Dedicated OS thread (~MB stack) spawn:thread { heavy() } h.join() void On JoinHandle ; blocks until spawn completes; consumes handle let h = spawn { … }; h.join() parallel for i in 0..n { ... } / parallel:task parallel loop Task pool (default); max = N , cpu = N% parallel for · parallel:task(max = 4) · cpu = N% · mode = … parallel:thread(...) for ... parallel loop OS thread fork-join per chunk parallel:thread(max = 4) progress for x in items { ... } loop + progress bar Optional label progress for defer expr scope-exit call (LIFO) Extended — prefer impl Drop defer · LIFO async fn … { … } promise / future handle Body on spawn:thread ; not on wasm32 async fn await handle blocks until complete Inside async fn or at call site await Examples: allow_extended · JoinHandle · random · date() · spawn · .join() · parallel for · progress · benchmark · defer · async/await

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: File directive: allow_extended
docs.body: File directive: allow_extended spawn , parallel for , async / await , defer , and related APIs live in Nyra’s Stable Extended tier. Put this line at the top of the file (before fn main or imports) when the unit intentionally uses those features: allow_extended What it does Why use it Marks the file as an Extended-tier program Documents intent for readers and reviewers Suppresses warning[W001] Extended-feature warnings are silenced in this file (useful when CI runs with stricter flags) One line per file Not per-function — applies to the whole compilation unit You do not need allow_extended for Core-only code (plain print , structs, loops, etc.). All spawn and parallel examples below include it. See also Concurrency → spawn and Roadmap (Core vs Extended). Name: allow_extended (first line of file) Explanation: Declares that this compilation unit intentionally uses Stable Extended APIs. Does not enable features by itself — it documents intent and integrates with stability warnings. Example: Without types With types allow_extended fn main() { parallel for i in 0..3 { print(i) } } allow_extended fn main() -> void { parallel for i in 0..3 { print(i) } } Output: Same as parallel for (iteration order non-deterministic). Without this line, Extended syntax still compiles in v1.39+ default builds; the directive is recommended in files that use spawn , parallel for , etc. Platform: spawn is unavailable on wasm32-wasi (compile error). Native Linux, macOS, and Windows only.

docs.title: Built-in methods
docs.heading: JoinHandle and .join()
docs.body: JoinHandle and .join() When spawn appears as an expression ( let h = spawn { … } ), the type of h is JoinHandle — an opaque handle to a background task or thread. It is not a numeric ID; you cannot print it or compare it. You either wait on it or drop it. Method Signature Effect .join() h.join() -> void Blocks the current thread until the spawned work finishes. Consumes the handle (move) — you cannot call .join() twice on the same variable. Form Syntax Wait for completion? Statement spawn { … } No — fire-and-forget; runtime detaches immediately Expression + join let h = spawn { … }; h.join() Yes — main waits at h.join() Drop without join let h = spawn { … } then scope ends No — handle dropped → detach (same as statement form) .join() takes no arguments . Works the same for spawn , spawn:task , and spawn:thread ; the compiler calls the correct runtime ( spawn_task_join vs spawn_join ) based on how the handle was created. See example .

docs.heading: String helpers
docs.body: String helpers Without types With types import "stdlib/builtins_string.ny" fn main() { let s = " Nyra Lang " print(String_toUpperCase(clone s)) print(String_toLowerCase(clone s)) print(String_includes(clone s, "Lang")) let parts = "a,b,c".split(",") print(parts.length()) print(String_replace("hello world", "world", "nyra")) print(trim(clone s)) } import "stdlib/builtins_string.ny" fn main() -> void { let s: string = " Nyra Lang " print(String_toUpperCase(clone s)) print(String_toLowerCase(clone s)) print(String_includes(clone s, "Lang")) let parts: VecStr = "a,b,c".split(",") print(parts.length()) print(String_replace("hello world", "world", "nyra")) print(trim(clone s)) } Output NYRA LANG 1 3 hello nyra Nyra Lang
